author: Catherine Bennett
title: cross_topic_1/train/0/train_2
assignment:
Write a satirical political commentary column of approximately 1,265 words, divided into one long lead section and two shorter satirical vignettes separated by bold subheadings.

**Lead section (~800 words):** Begin with a magazine profile of an aging political leader—admiring, optimistic, framed as a tale of reinvention—and immediately undercut it by noting the leader's current political misfortunes: lost by-elections, ridicule, and fading support. Use the contrast between the magazine's lead time and the pace of political decline as a running joke about journalistic hubris. Trace the leader's brief arc from media darling to embarrassment by quoting (or inventing) earlier press praise—words like "forensic," "relentless," "incisive"—and then showing how those same qualities curdled into liabilities. Reference the leader's parliamentary predecessors (at least two prior leaders of the same party who were similarly discarded) and note the pattern: each one hailed as a savior, each one swiftly abandoned. Introduce the metaphor of disposable consumer goods—specifically a washing machine past its warranty—and extend it to the party's leadership turnover, suggesting the next leader will have even less time. End with an environmental joke about the accumulating waste of discarded leaders.

**Second section (~200 words), bold subheading:** A short satirical piece linking elite private schooling to leftist radicalism. Start from recent obituaries of a prominent left-wing journalist who attended such a school. Argue, tongue in cheek, that the British left owes its existence to the cruelty of the house system and that softening these schools may threaten future dissent. Reference a contemporary musician (or similar figure) whose anti-establishment art was forged in a moderately strict school environment. Conclude by noting the irony that a government minister's promotion of competitive school sport may, through this logic, end up producing more revolutionaries for the far left.

**Third section (~200 words), bold subheading:** A satirical response to a report on interracial friendship and social integration. Begin by tallying your own social circle and finding it grievously lacking—not just in racial diversity but in every conceivable category of person (Welsh friends, estate agent friends, baronet friends, etc.). Wonder how adults are supposed to acquire the mandated diversity of friendships: friendship leave? A lottery system in which citizens are randomly assigned twenty new friends? End with a darkly comic punchline about who one unlucky citizen might be assigned.

Throughout: maintain a dry, knowing, self-mocking tone. Use invented or paraphrased press quotes freely. Reference contemporary political figures, sketch writers, and magazine culture. The piece should read as a single columnist's weekly output—thematically linked by the absurdity of institutions (parties, schools, friendships) that promise renewal but deliver only more of the same waste and disappointment.

author: Catherine Bennett
title: cross_topic_1/train/0/train_5
assignment:
Write a satirical political commentary (approximately 1,360 words) that mocks the way politicians co-opt the language of therapy and emotional healing to shut down scrutiny and declare victory. The essay should have two main sections.

**Opening and Thesis:** Begin by referencing Susie Orbach's "Antidote" campaign to bring "emotional literacy" into British public life, noting that Tessa Jowell was a signatory. Use this as a springboard to argue, ironically, that the campaign has succeeded — politicians now speak in the language of marriage counselors. Cite a specific political moment (e.g., a prime minister receiving an apology and declaring it lets everyone "draw a line and move on"). Catalogue how multiple political figures — the PM, his press secretary, the culture secretary — all deploy phrases like "move on" and "closure" in coordinated fashion, as though running a group therapy session for the nation. The thesis: this therapeutic vocabulary is a weapon, not genuine emotional honesty, and means nothing more than "I've won, so bugger off."

**First Section — The Therapy Satire:** Adopt the persona of a citizen-patient who, were they to lie on the prime minister's couch, would describe feeling "stuck," betrayed, and unable to trust. Satirically recommend an actual-style self-help book on forgiveness and moving on (cite a plausible author and title). Use the book's framework to mock the politicians: note that the person who hurt you is "getting on with their life," just as the PM is already launching his next inquiry. Suggest the book's strategy of recalling "good times" with the perpetrator — then list broken political promises as those good times. Conclude the section by questioning why politicians appropriate counseling language at all, noting that real therapists define "moving on" and "closure" as laborious processes, not declarations you impose on others. Observe that "closure" is the most abject of counseling clichés, and that you cannot claim it on someone else's behalf.

**Second Section — The Bureaucratic Satire:** Pivot to a public figure presented as the government's guarantor of fairness in some appointment process (e.g., the BBC chairmanship). Have the culture secretary repeatedly invoke this person as the answer to all questions about the selection's integrity, while deflecting any inquiry into who oversees the overseer. Profile this person satirically: enumerate their absurdly long list of titles, board positions, honorary professorships, consulting roles, and salaries. Mock their leadership philosophy by quoting their actual-style maxims — metaphors about flying at 30,000 feet and knowing when to "land and walk," lists of "unspoken employee requests" like "acknowledge the greatness within me," homemade poems about life as a circus, and leadership principles borrowed from a famous general that include gems like "don't let adverse facts stand in the way of a good decision." End with a dry punchline noting that the culture secretary would remind us that judging any of this is "entirely a matter" for the overseer.

**Tone:** Dry, witty, ironic throughout. Use the essay's own therapeutic and bureaucratic vocabulary as ammunition against its subjects. Let the satire do the work — no explicit moralizing. Structure with a bold subheading for the second section.

author: Catherine Bennett
title: cross_topic_1/train/0/train_6
assignment:
Write a satirical political commentary of approximately 1,350 words consisting of two linked sections, both mocking the gap between elite political/cultural gestures and ordinary public reality.

**Section One (~1,000 words):** Critique a government-launched public consultation initiative (framed as a "national conversation" or "big dialogue"). Open by quoting the leader's preemptive warning that cynics will sneer at the exercise. Then eavesdrop on the official website's contributions, revealing them to be overwhelmingly fawning and suspiciously uniform in their praise — contributors thanking the government for their prosperity, contrasting present contentment with the misery of a previous opposition era, and offering only the most anodyne "constructive criticism." Weave in a direct quotation from Orwell's *1984* (the party history textbook passage about pre-Revolution London) presented as if it were one of the website contributions, letting the reader spot the parallel. Note the conspicuous absence of any engagement with the major political issues of the day (war, terrorism, public services, constitutional reform, etc.), and observe that the only complaints permitted through are either hopelessly parochial personal petitions or blame-shifted toward citizens rather than government. Compare the website to Stalin's Potemkin villages and to a pre-1989 edition of *Pravda*. Close by wondering whether the propagandistic tone reflects active censorship or simple public apathy, and end with the observation that the initiative's name itself echoes Orwell's Big Brother — giving cynics their field day.

**Section Two (~350 words, under a separate subheading):** Pivot to a related cultural story — a public intellectual announcing they will return a state honour (e.g., an MBE). Satirize the gesture by inventing an absurd municipal infrastructure for disposing of unwanted honours (a recycling scheme). Quote the intellectual's admission that "little people" — nurses, dinner ladies, community workers — still value such recognition, and mock the condescension implicit in that framing. Reference Benjamin Zephaniah's well-known refusal of an honour. Close by proposing, with deadpan irony, that future honours lists be accompanied by supplementary "returned honours" lists so that conscience-stricken ordinary people can briefly experience the feeling of being important — the same feeling the intellectual columnists already enjoy through their newspaper platforms.

**Tone throughout:** Dry, acidic, archly literate. Use invented contributor names and quotations to populate the consultation website. Allusions to make: Orwell's *1984*, Stalin's Potemkin villages, *Pravda*, Benjamin Zephaniah's honour refusal, Leona Helmsley's "only little people pay taxes." The piece should function both as media criticism and as a broader argument that managed public discourse and performative elite dissent are two sides of the same coin of political theatre.

author: Jonathan Freedland
title: cross_topic_1/train/3/train_27
assignment:
Write a year-end column in which you assess your own forecasting record from the previous twelve months. Begin with a self-deprecating introduction that frames the exercise as a performance review — noting that schools, hospitals, and corporations all undergo such appraisals, and that a columnist should be no exception. Explain that exactly one year ago you published a set of "reckless predictions" and that now is the moment to see where you hit the mark and where you missed badly.

Structure the body as a series of case-by-case evaluations, moving through roughly six topics:

1. **A judicial inquiry into government conduct** — You correctly predicted the leader would survive and that a media organization would be faulted, but you wrongly expected the inquiry to "spread the pain evenly" across both government and media. Instead, the judge inflicted damage on only one side and ruled more boldly than you anticipated on the central controversy.

2. **A national election (US)** — You called the winner right but badly misidentified his opponent, having backed a different primary candidate who never made it to the general election.

3. **An ongoing military occupation** — You rightly predicted continued violence and dismissed optimistic talk following a dictator's capture, but you wrongly anticipated that the occupying power would timetable elections and begin withdrawing troops for domestic political reasons.

4. **An intractable regional conflict (Israel-Palestine)** — You correctly sensed that the incumbent leader's old approach would be seen as futile, but you did not foresee that he himself would initiate a new strategy. You also wrongly predicted that corruption scandals would force his resignation.

5. **Domestic opposition politics** — You predicted the opposition leader would have a solid year, and while his party technically topped a nationwide poll, the vote share was historically dismal.

6. **Domestic figures** — You correctly predicted that a maverick politician would be readmitted to his party and re-elected as a city mayor, and that a powerful cabinet figure would remain in his post and continue waiting.

After the case-by-case review, step back and extract lessons. Identify two: first, that you should have distrusted the conventional wisdom of political insiders (using the wrong primary prediction as evidence); second — and more surprisingly for a journalist — that you were insufficiently cynical. You expected institutions of accountability to function fairly and predictably, but they did not. Articulate a broader theme that defined the year: **unaccountability** — powerful figures surviving despite clear evidence of failure or wrongdoing, with no senior consequences.

Conclude by stating the two lessons the year taught you: don't expect accountability mechanisms to work, and don't make predictions. End with a humorous, unexpected example of the world's turbulence — a cabinet minister caught in an improbable personal scandal that nobody could have foreseen.

Target approximately 1,000 words. Adopt a tone that is wry, self-critical, and occasionally sharp — a columnist comfortable both dishing out judgment and absorbing it.

author: Jonathan Freedland
title: cross_topic_1/train/3/train_31
assignment:
Write an opinion essay (approximately 1205 words) arguing that the wealthy receive preferential treatment from government, that existing political and regulatory mechanisms cannot fix this problem, and that the remedy lies in anti-discrimination law—specifically Article 14 of the European Convention on Human Rights, now incorporated into British law via the Human Rights Act.

**Introduction (roughly 3 paragraphs):** Open by cataloguing a series of official exonerations in which public officials were cleared of wrongdoing despite circumstances that smell of favoritism. Reference at least three cases: a cash-for-passports inquiry (involving billionaire donors and a senior cabinet minister) that found nothing "improper"; a parliamentary sleaze committee that cleared a minister of nearly all charges while declining to punish the one minor violation it did find; and a Downing Street intervention in a planning application by a billionaire arms broker, dismissed as mere concern over "timing." Adopt a tone of dry irony—the guardians of civic probity have spoken, all is well. Then pivot sharply: if the rules say nothing wrong happened, why doesn't it feel right? Pose this as the essay's central question.

**Section 1 — The Common-Sense Diagnosis (roughly 2 paragraphs):** Answer the question directly: forget the inquiries; these are all versions of the same story. Someone with money got special treatment. Use the passport case to illustrate: even the exoneration report itself documents that the billionaire's application was personally championed by the head of the immigration directorate, that residency rules were bent to accelerate processing, and that a roster of former prime ministers and senior ministers lobbied on the applicants' behalf. The report calls this "proper"; the public knows what it really is. Reinforce the pattern with two further examples: a Formula One boss whose large donation to the governing party was followed by an exemption from a tobacco advertising ban, and a party treasurer whose massive donations were followed by a peerage. Conclude that the officials' definition of "proper" is itself the problem—we need a new one.

**Section 2 — Why the Obvious Solutions Fail (roughly 2 paragraphs):** Consider, then reject, the standard remedies. First, state funding of political parties: this would free politicians from groveling to millionaires, but it wouldn't have prevented the passport case, because the billionaires in question were donating to a cultural project (the Millennium Dome), not to a party. State funding also carries its own risks, like starving new political movements of resources. Second, better regulation by parliamentary committees: but the committee that just investigated the minister had to drop most of its charges because he refused to cooperate—they were powerless. The regulatory route is structurally inadequate.

**Section 3 — The Legal Solution (roughly 4 paragraphs):** Propose a more radical remedy drawn from the law. Frame the core offense as a violation of the principle that everyone should be equal under the law—there is one rule for the rich and another for everyone else. Draw an explicit parallel to the United States: the 14th Amendment's "equal protection of the laws" clause has underpinned civil rights litigation from Brown v. Board of Education (1954) onward, allowing citizens to petition courts to outlaw preferential treatment for one group over another.

Reveal that Britain now has its own analog: Article 14 of the European Convention on Human Rights, absorbed into domestic law through the Human Rights Act. That article bars discrimination on grounds of sex, race, colour, and "any other status"—and "other status" could encompass wealth. Illustrate with hypothetical scenarios: two ordinary citizens petitioning the courts to ask why their passport application wasn't handled as swiftly as a billionaire's; a small developer complaining that his planning decision wasn't expedited like a billionaire's; a modest business owner claiming he was denied a policy exemption granted to a wealthy donor.

Cite a leading human rights lawyer (reference Keir Starmer by name) confirming that discrimination against those with less money could fall within Article 14's prohibitions, provided the claimant could amass evidence and prove the cases were genuinely comparable in every respect bar wealth. Note that at minimum such a challenge would force the authority to explain and justify its differential treatment.

**Conclusion (roughly 1 paragraph):** Close by observing that experts always said the Human Rights Act would make a big difference. In the United States, the Bill of Rights has long been used to advance civil rights causes that politicians found too politically difficult. Now it is Britain's turn. For those sick of fast tracks and special favors for the rich, politicians are too compromised to be the solution—it is time to go to court.

**Tone and Style Notes:** Write in the voice of a newspaper columnist—accessible, argumentative, laced with sardonic understatement in the opening, building to genuine polemical force by the end. Use concrete named examples throughout (specific politicians, specific billionaires, specific legal cases). The essay should read as a call to action: the political system cannot police itself on this question; only the courts can.

author: Martin Kettle
title: cross_topic_1/train/4/train_34
assignment:
Write a piece of approximately 1,100 words making the case that a significant but forgotten left-leaning prime minister deserves rescue from historical oblivion. The subject is Sir Henry Campbell-Bannerman ("CB"), the Liberal prime minister whose 1906 landslide election victory inaugurated the longest continuous period of left-of-center government in British history — nearly 17 years, ending with the fall of the Lloyd George coalition in 1922.

Open by situating the reader: there have been three great left-wing electoral landslides since the Victorian era — New Labour in 1997 (repeated 2001), Labour in 1945, and the Liberal landslide of 1906 — won respectively by Tony Blair, Clement Attlee, and a man almost no one remembers. Introduce Campbell-Bannerman as that forgotten figure.

Note the centenary context: it has been roughly a hundred years since CB took office and launched the Liberal era in government. Observe that this 17-year span remains unmatched by any other British government of the left, and that while the era is associated with Asquith and Lloyd George, the pivotal 1906 victory belonged to CB alone.

Sketch his personality appealingly: cite Margot Asquith calling him "essentially a bon vivant, a boulevardier and a humorist," and note the rarity of such traits in prime ministers since Disraeli. Draw on his biographer John Wilson, who described CB as easy-going, lacking ambition, and inclined to let well alone. Emphasize that he kept politics in proportion, was no workaholic, and never bore grudges.

Then pivot: do not mistake this for laziness or empty pragmatism. CB was not an intellectual but possessed deep, unwavering Liberal convictions, comparable to his lifelong love of Balzac and Flaubert. Quote his 1898 speech defining Liberalism — self-government, trust in mankind's general sense, opposition to artificial privilege, and laws that avoid aggravating inequality and secure every person a chance at a good and useful life. Note that this formulation still reads well over a century later.

Develop three main arguments for admiring him:

First, his principled stand on the Boer War. The Liberal Party nearly split over it, with much of the hierarchy under Lord Rosebery supporting the war and radicals opposing it. As new leader, CB initially sought a middle course but ultimately delivered the era's most important anti-war speech in 1901, condemning the Salisbury government's "methods of barbarism."

Second, his pragmatic radicalism toward the emerging Labour Party. CB liked Labour figures, sympathized with their grievances, and within months of taking office delivered the 1906 Trades Disputes Act. He fought off Asquith's efforts to weaken the bill and pushed a stronger version through cabinet, making it the cornerstone of 20th-century labor law.

Third, his political skill. He led from the center, rebuilt the party from a bad defeat in 1900 to a massive win in 1906 — overturning a Tory majority of 134 into a Liberal majority of 130, one of the largest swings ever. He won by exploiting his opponents' weaknesses (the protectionist tariff campaign) without overpromising. Most colleagues thought his strategy wrong; the result vindicated him.

Assess his government's performance in office: it outperformed expectations. Had the Tory-dominated House of Lords not blocked legislation, CB would have achieved education reform, abolition of the multiple franchise, and trade union law reform within his first year. Even so, ministers like Haldane (army reform) and Lloyd George (merchant shipping) proved genuine reformers.

Address how his career ended. Cite Enoch Powell's dictum that all political careers end in failure, noting it was written about Joseph Chamberlain. Observe that the axiom does not apply to CB except in the literal sense that he died in Downing Street in early 1908, having resigned the premiership on his deathbed in favor of Asquith. Frame this as going out at the top.

Conclude with the theme of oblivion. Mention Andrew Bonar Law as a rival for prime ministerial obscurity, rescued by Robert Blake's biography "The Unknown Prime Minister." Suggest Alec Douglas-Home may someday recede further into the shadows. End by asserting that, for now, the title of Britain's unknown prime minister unjustly belongs to the admirable and neglected Henry Campbell-Bannerman.

author: Martin Kettle
title: cross_topic_1/train/4/train_37
assignment:
Write an essay of approximately 1,200 words defending the reliability of opinion polls and arguing that apparent contradictions between polls usually stem from differences in how questions are framed rather than from polling unreliability.

**Opening:** Begin with two personal anecdotes set on election days. In the first, on the morning of the 1983 general election, you tell a Labour ward secretary in Hackney that the Tories will win by more than 100 seats; he insists Labour will win by 20. You were right—Thatcher returned with a majority of 144. In the second, on election day 1997, you ask the Labour leader what he thinks the result will be; he says maybe a win by 50, and you say more like 150. In both cases, your "secret" was simply believing what the opinion polls said.

**Second section:** Extend the argument to the present. When asked about the upcoming US presidential election (November 2, 2004), you say John Kerry is winning—not out of partisanship, but because that is what the polls currently show, especially in battleground states. Include a jab at hostile right-wing American email correspondents who call you names. Then add an important caveat: saying Kerry is ahead in the polls is not the same as predicting he will win. Polls are snapshots, not predictions; they have margins of error; the election is months away. All you can say is that it's close and Kerry is narrowly ahead.

**Third section:** Pivot to domestic UK politics. Acknowledge that poll-believers have some explaining to do given two seemingly contradictory recent polls: a Guardian-ICM poll found Iraq at the bottom of a list of prospective election issues, while an FT-Mori poll found defence and foreign affairs were the most important issues facing the country. Note that respected observers like Peter Preston have expressed confusion at this. Argue that the confusion is more apparent than real, and that it matters for civic good to stamp out the lazy conceits that polls can't be trusted or can be made to say anything.

**Fourth section:** Explain the resolution. Different polls ask different questions. ICM asked respondents to pick from a prompted list which issues would be most important in deciding how they vote; just 12% chose "Iraq," while the NHS topped the list at 59%. Mori asked an unprompted question about the most important issue facing Britain today; "defence/foreign affairs/international terrorism" came top at 24% (38% when including "other important issues"). The polls are not contradictory—they are compatible. It is perfectly possible to see a subject as the most important national issue while not weighting it heavily in your voting decision.

**Fifth section:** Provide a historical parallel. In the 1980s, polls regularly showed unemployment was the most important issue facing the country and that Labour had the best policies on it, yet people voted Conservative because on voting-day issues like taxes and economic competence, the Tories had the advantage. Argue a similar dynamic is at work today: foreign affairs and terrorism may be the most important national issue, but when it comes to elections, people vote on health, education, and personal concerns. Conclude that this is why Labour will win the next election—just as the polls say they will.

author: Martin Kettle
title: cross_topic_1/train/4/train_39
assignment:
Write an essay of approximately 1,140 words analyzing the tension between loyalty and honest judgment in political leadership, using the relationship between Tony Blair and the United States as your central case study.

Begin with a reflection on how difficult it is for true believers to publicly criticize causes they hold dear. Establish that conscience and loyalty are perpetually at war, and that this tension never produces an easy winner. Introduce Tony Blair’s deep belief in the importance of the US alliance as a classic case of the perils of undifferentiated loyalty. Frame his current difficulties (around Iraq) as a struggle between enduring instincts and threatening realities.

In the first section, explore Blair’s view that British domestic politics operate “downstream” from America. Argue that he saw Bill Clinton’s 1992 election and 1996 re-election as preconditions for Labour’s 1997 victory, because a Democratic White House made Labour appear aligned with the grain of history. Then explain how George W. Bush’s election in 2000 posed a serious challenge, and why Blair rushed to be the first foreign visitor to Camp David in early 2001—partly to prevent Conservative leaders (William Hague, and later Iain Duncan Smith) from claiming to be the party in touch with the new America.

In the next section, trace how Blair’s belief widened from domestic to international politics. Describe his emerging doctrine: that the US is the indispensable nation for settling global problems, and that the Anglo-American relationship is the catalyst that keeps America engaged rather than isolationist. Note the ambiguity of how much Blair truly believes in the “special relationship” mystique versus his evident self-identification as a European. Include the anecdote of Blair at a 1998 White House dinner with Clinton, quoting Harry Hopkins’s biblical words to Churchill—“Whither thou goest I will go… Thy people shall be my people, and thy God my God. Even to the end.”—as evidence of where his heart lay.

Then pivot to the contrast between the Clinton and Bush eras. Acknowledge that Blair sometimes struggled to secure US engagement even under Clinton (Kosovo), but stress that Bush presents a fundamentally different problem. Cite Frances Fitzgerald’s observation in the New York Review of Books that the Bush administration broke with the internationalist premises accepted by every postwar administration except Reagan’s first. Develop the argument that Bush has rarely defined his foreign-policy goals in concrete terms, speaking vaguely of threats and US-defined security solutions, exemplified by his campaign remark: “We’re not so sure who the they are, but we know they’re there.”

Next, address how Bush’s subordinates—Dick Cheney, Donald Rumsfeld, and especially John Bolton—have set an agenda that repudiates collective action and alliances. Quote Bolton’s claim that “there is no such thing as the UN” and that the international community is merely what the US can lead when it suits its interests. Describe the Pentagon’s shift from a “threat-based strategy” to a “capabilities-based approach,” allowing it to request military spending without justifying it against any identifiable enemy, using Rumsfeld’s language of defending “against the unknown.”

Conclude by arguing that this reality constantly undermines Blair’s efforts to portray the Bush administration as a willing partner in the moral internationalist order he championed in his Brighton conference speech and reiterated to the TUC. Acknowledge sympathy for Blair: he believes in the right things and is trying to exert necessary influence. But argue that he earns grief at home and in Washington because of a profound disjunction between what he wants to believe about the administration and what is actually true. End by returning to your opening theme: that this administration has discarded the rules Blair wants to play by, and that rather than confronting that reality, Blair pretends it does not exist—the classic error of the over-loyalist, who risks letting loyalty override clarity and truth.

author: Catherine Bennett
title: cross_topic_1/validation/0/validation_2
assignment:
Write a satirical political commentary (approximately 1,335 words) examining a botched government licensing decision that exposes the gap between a ruling party's opposition rhetoric and its actions in power. Use the following structure and beats:

**Introduction:** Open with a "glass half-full / half-empty" framing applied to a binary outcome where both options are objectionable — one side represented by a pompous public figure known for self-branding, the other by a profit-seeking corporate operator widely despised. Address the political leader whose own efficacy-evaluation metaphor you are co-opting. Note the darkly comic quality of the loser's public emotional display.

**Section 1 — Opposition Promises:** Remind readers of the governing party's earlier, righteous denunciations of the corporate operator when they were in opposition. Quote or paraphrase specific promises made by the leader and key cabinet ministers to replace the profit-making operator with a non-profit model, including comparisons to other countries and rhetoric about serving "the people."

**Section 2 — The Dome Parallel:** Draw an explicit comparison to another contemporaneous government fiasco (a grand public project). Argue that this licensing saga is in some ways even worse, because the government had actually opposed the corporate operator in opposition. Identify the shared feature: no one of political consequence will be held accountable. Describe how both failures are being framed as forces of nature too complex for any human to manage.

**Section 3 — The Commission's Incompetence:** Question why the responsible minister appointed a commission so conspicuously lacking in relevant expertise. Single out the commission's chair as a particular mystery of appointment. Describe how, with only two bids before them, the commission dismissed both but gave only one bidder — the pompous public figure — a second chance, a piece of favoritism "that would have most toddlers reeling with sympathetic indignation."

**Section 4 — Judicial Review and Non-Accountability:** Recount how the spurned corporate operator successfully sought judicial review. Note the judge's harsh language ("conspicuously unfair," "unlawful," "an abuse of power"). Express astonishment that the chair did not immediately resign, and was initially supported by the minister. Describe the chair's eventual grudging departure, attributed to "media vilification," and the remaining commissioners' decision to stay on under a new, successor chair who acknowledges they are discredited but insists there is no time to replace them.

**Section 5 — Litigation as Governance:** Note the losing bidder's history of and appetite for litigation. Argue that the entire saga has, in the absence of competent political leadership, been effectively settled by lawyers — from the treasury solicitors who approved the initial unfair decision, to the corporate QC who overturned it, to the city firm overseeing the final deliberations (which has coincidentally worked for all interested parties).

**Conclusion:** Deliver a satirical punch: given the choice between the incompetent political appointees and the brainy lawyers, why not just abandon the pretense of democratic government and let the lawyers run everything? Then note, with a final twist, that the current leader is already doing exactly that — appointing lords and lawyers to govern — and simply hasn't finished yet.

**Tone throughout:** Bitter, mocking, darkly comic. Use sarcasm as the primary rhetorical engine. Address the political leader directly at key moments. Treat all parties — the self-branding mogul, the corporate profiteers, the incompetent commissioners, the weak minister — with equal contempt. Allusions to Dickens (the death of Little Nell) and general literary melodrama should flavor the satire. Include a brief coda of related links and statements as if for a newspaper website.

author: George Monbiot
title: cross_topic_1/validation/1/validation_5
assignment:
Write an investigative opinion piece (approximately 794 words) exposing a government minister's conflict of interest, structured as follows:

**Introduction:** Open with an apparently minor scandal—a planning minister (Nick Raynsford) allegedly covered up a decision to allow a listed building in Oxford to be moved for a business school funded by arms broker Wafic Said. Establish his alibi (he was in Egypt leading a trade mission) and frame it as unchallengeable. Then pivot: the real question is what a planning minister was doing on a trade delegation to Egypt at all. Thesis: the alibi reveals a more serious problem than the alleged crime.

**Section 1 — The Trade Mission:** Detail the delegation of 27 corporations (Balfour Beatty, Enron, Laing, Mott MacDonald, Thames Water, WS Atkins) and their visit to Egypt's contested Toshka project—a $90bn Nile diversion scheme condemned by both environmentalists and financial consultants. Emphasize the irony: this is exactly the kind of project that, if proposed in Britain, would fall under this minister's regulatory scrutiny.

**Section 2 — The Norwegian Anomaly:** Note that the major contract winner was Kvaerner (Norwegian), not a British firm. Explain this by revealing Kvaerner had seconded more employees into the Department of the Environment than any other company—suggesting corporate capture of the regulator.

**Section 3 — The Structural Conflict:** Explain Raynsford's dual role: minister for planning (regulator of development) AND minister for construction (promoter of development). Argue he routinely resolves this conflict in industry's favor. Quote him telling *Building* magazine his job is "creating a climate in which the industry can do well."

**Section 4 — Pattern of Industry Favoritism:** Catalog examples of him undermining government policy on industry's behalf: sponsoring a PR campaign ("Minerals 98") for quarrying firms to lobby against a roadstone tax; arguing against out-of-town parking taxes at Labour's 1998 local government conference; promising the British Council of Shopping Centres to speed up retail schemes; watering down energy efficiency rules for new homes as "an undue burden on builders."

**Section 5 — The Ilisu Dam:** Present the gravest allegation: Raynsford allegedly persuaded Tony Blair to back the Ilisu dam in Turkey—a project causing ethnic cleansing of 78,000 Kurds. Connect this to a September 1999 trade mission to Turkey led by Raynsford, accompanying a Balfour Beatty–fronted consortium seeking £4bn in earthquake-rebuilding contracts. Balfour Beatty also hopes to build the Ilisu dam. Three months later, the government announced it was "minded to grant" export credit. Pose the question: were British contracts made conditional on dam support?

**Conclusion:** Return to the Oxford business school. Deliver the real scandal: everything Raynsford says about it is probably true—there was nothing unusual about the decision. The outrage is that turning a blind eye to environmental and heritage destruction on behalf of the construction industry happens every week, by the very man supposed to police it. End on this structural indictment.

author: Hugo Young
title: cross_topic_1/validation/2/validation_11
assignment:
Write an opinion essay arguing that the death of ideology in politics has made governing both more necessary and harder to explain. Begin by noting that all parties now treat ideology as a dirty word, but observe that without it, politics becomes messier and harder for the public to follow, since the old left-right simplifications no longer apply. Argue that public understanding has not caught up with post-ideological reality—people still interpret reforms through outdated ideological frames.

Use the example of a center-left government's plans for public service delivery (health and education) being misread as privatization. Criticize the government's first term for trying to please everyone with contradictory messaging—reassuring business with talk of competition while telling its base nothing fundamental would change. Note that its guiding philosophy, "what works," was adopted before it actually knew what worked.

Clarify that enlisting private-sector skills for surgical centres or failing schools is not the same as privatization, and that contestability is standard practice across continental European public sectors, not an assault on public-service principles. Argue that very little of the public sector can actually be run by the private sector anyway.

Frame a major speech by the prime minister as the pivotal moment of clarification—the most important since re-election. Note that while the headline message is a refusal to "flinch" from union opposition, the speech is sober rather than militant, reflecting a late awakening to the danger that public services might be seen to fail. Reference the contrast with Thatcher's combative style and suggest this speech comes from the last chance saloon of British social democracy.

Identify three signals the speech sends. First, there is no single grand blueprint—different problems require different answers; cite examples involving local education authorities rated successful by inspection and new surgical centres supplementing NHS hospitals. Second, only a social-democratic government can be trusted to reform public services without simply enriching private business and harming the disadvantaged; reference a concurrent conservative leadership contest where most candidates now embrace public services, but one represents the hard right's minimal-state instincts. Third, acknowledge the challenge may be impossible to fully meet—public expectations are high, the glass is always at risk of appearing half empty, and a global downturn could threaten the budget surplus needed for investment.

Conclude by noting that the prime minister has begun the defining stage of his project without glamour or spin, and that this alone is worth recognizing and supporting.

author: Hugo Young
title: cross_topic_1/validation/2/validation_12
assignment:
Write an opinion essay (approximately 1,150 words) critiquing a government's abandonment of its stated anti-racist principles in the realm of immigration policy. The essay should examine how ministers who came to power professing deep commitment to racial equality have quietly enacted regulations that institutionalize ethnic discrimination.

**Thesis:** Power corrupts moral sensibility not through deliberate conspiracy but through righteous self-belief—ministers become unable to see that they are doing precisely what they once condemned.

**Structure and Beats:**

*Introduction:* Open at a transitional political moment (between terms, before a new mandate erases memory). Frame the piece as an audit of what power has done to ministers' moral perception—not just hardened arteries, but closed gates of self-awareness.

*Section 1 – The Stated Ideal:* Establish the government's founding anti-racist credentials. Reference specific early actions (abolishing a prior rule targeting migrant spouses; convening a landmark inquiry into a racially motivated murder). Emphasize that leading figures treated racial justice as an article of faith, making it "unimaginable" they would act otherwise.

*Section 2 – The First Betrayal:* Reveal that the government passed race relations legislation that explicitly exempted the immigration service from anti-discrimination rules. Note a ministerial order authorizing differential treatment of unspecified nationalities. Record the minister's dismissive response to criticism (calling it a "technicality," labeling the attack "bizarre").

*Section 3 – The Escalation:* Detail a second, more specific order listing targeted ethnic groups by name (Kurd, Roma, Albanian, Tamil, Pontic Greek, Somali, Afghan). Explain that these groups are to face "more rigorous examination" as classes, presumed more likely to be illegal immigrants or bogus asylum-seekers.

*Section 4 – The Vicious Absurdity:* Highlight that several named groups have no corresponding passport or nation-state (Kurds, Roma), meaning identification rests on appearance and inference. Argue this enshrines a racial-profiling logic into statute. Note the cruel sub-clause: if information needed for an application isn't available in a language the applicant understands, officials need not provide it in one they do.

*Section 5 – Two Phases of Deception:* Phase one: during the bill's passage, ministers claimed the power was only to discriminate *in favor* of groups like Kosovan refugees. Call this a lie. Phase two: after enactment, the responsible minister casually admitted this merely formalized long-standing practice, expressing flippancy that anyone would object.

*Section 6 – Three Conclusions:*
- First: ethnic selection is now systematized and official. A "race monitor" exists but only retrospectively and without examining individual cases—a PR figurehead.
- Second: this is political cowardice. The government is caving to a hardline opposition figure's rhetoric on asylum, collaborating in a discourse that stigmatizes asylum-seeking itself as presumptively fraudulent.
- Third: something has rotted in the ruling party. They have become as bad as their predecessors while losing the intellectual capacity to recognize it. The party barely protested. The lead minister, a self-styled anti-racist, genuinely cannot see the contradiction.

*Conclusion:* Diagnose the deeper corruption: not self-interested conspiracy but a wall of righteous certainty—the belief that because of who they are, they can do no wrong. End with the judgment that this law is a disgrace, and the bitter irony that perhaps only a party convinced of its own anti-racism would have the nerve to smuggle such ethnic punishment past the line separating decency from shame.

**Tone:** Outraged but controlled, analytical rather than purely emotive. Use specific policy details as evidence. Address the reader directly at moments. Maintain a sense of moral gravity without sermonizing.

author: Hugo Young
title: cross_topic_1/test/2/test_13
assignment:
Write an opinion essay of approximately 1165 words arguing that the politics of law and order is entirely driven by spin rather than substance. Your thesis: in no other area of political conflict does impression so completely eclipse reality, and both major parties manipulate statistics to create impressions rather than confront genuine problems.

**Introduction:** Open with the bold declaration that law and order politics is "entirely about spin"—not partly, entirely. Acknowledge that some impressions happen to be correct (crime has doubled over 20 years; the public does genuinely want heavier sentencing), but insist that the raw material politicians handle is propaganda, not fact.

**Section 1 — Statistical Warfare:** Contrast two competing claims from the home secretary and the shadow home secretary, each asserting their party's crime record is the best in decades. Note that both claims are relatively defensible depending on what years you measure, what categories you include, and how you shade the figures. The voter cannot possibly parse this. Cite a specific audit of the government (name two journalist authors and a publisher) to illustrate the mixed picture: burglary down significantly, car theft down, violent crime down modestly, but street robbery up, clear-up rates stagnant, and convictions up. Conclude that sophistication, however true, will always be outgunned by simplicity, however false.

**Section 2 — Public Ignorance:** Reference a national crime survey showing the public believes judges are too lenient and out of touch. Then reveal that the same public is startlingly uninformed: over half underestimate actual sentences by at least 30%. They believe half of convicted rapists go to prison when the real figure is 97%. Similar misconceptions surround burglary and mugging sentences. These grotesque misperceptions are the foundation of the judiciary's "soft touch" reputation—a image brainlessly cultivated by the tabloid press, which then seeps into ministerial critique and policy.

**Section 3 — Policy History and Current Proposals:** Briefly survey the legislative record. Note that the previous Conservative government passed a criminal justice act every year until later statutes began undoing earlier ones. Assess the current government's mixed record: curfew orders and anti-social behaviour orders, though reasonable in design, have failed in practice. Evaluate the latest reform package—allowing judges to take account of previous convictions in sentencing—as a rational idea addressing the analysis that a small population of roughly 100,000 repeat offenders commits most crime. Acknowledge it will spin well, and that the opposition will spin against it just as easily.

**Section 4 — Three Unaddressed Challenges:** Present three conclusions that spinning fails to address:

First, the best policies cost money. Price the current package at a specific figure and question whether it is sufficient to fund new custodial regimes, accelerated court hearings, and increased police manpower. Argue that a larger police force is the single most persuasive remedy across all the confusing statistics. Note the home secretary's conspicuous failure to meet recruitment promises and question whether the Treasury is genuinely committed or whether this is mere propaganda timed to an upcoming election (name a specific date).

Second, there is a hole at the heart of the crime agenda. While politicians offer drugs as an explanation for crime, they refuse to address drug laws as a focus for reform despite expert pressure. Argue that decriminalizing cannabis would eliminate a category of crime at a stroke and free up police and courts. The spinners dare not look at it.

Third, prisons keep multiplying. Present two statistics: Britain has the highest imprisonment rate in Europe, and England and Wales rank joint first among industrialised nations in crime as experienced by victims. Note that no accountable politician will connect these two trends. Reference recent clinical and passionate critiques of the prison system from the lord chief justice, the inspector of prisons, and the director of the prison service. The electoral auctioneers dare not change the terms of debate.

**Conclusion:** Explain that spinners can only appeal to the lowest denominator of opinion, however ignorant, and that the spinner always, more or less, lies. End with the argument that the beginning of wisdom in the politics of law and order would be for someone to start the arduous business of addressing the truth—since nobody can any longer sort one spinner from another.

**Tone:** Sharp, analytical, slightly cynical about political spin but earnest about the need for truth. British broadsheet opinion-column register. Use specific statistics, named politicians, and referenced publications throughout to ground the argument in the texture of real political debate.

author: Hugo Young
title: cross_topic_1/test/2/test_16
assignment:
Write an essay of approximately 1165 words analyzing the final stages of the Northern Ireland peace process, focusing on the deadlock over IRA arms decommissioning. 

Your thesis should be that while both sides have made sincere and substantial concessions, the IRA must make a symbolic gesture of decommissioning to prove their commitment to peace, as mere promises of non-violence are insufficient in the real world.

Structure the essay as follows:

Introduction: Open by noting the disbelief that the peace process might fail after coming so far. Explain that the end game is the hardest part because the conflict is no longer about power or religion, but about attitudes toward mutual surrender. State that two forms of "real sincerity" are now ranged against each other, which is why the challenge may defeat them.

Section 1: The Republican Position. Argue that on the republican side, there is a sincere belief the war is over. Mention that Sinn Fein-IRA, led by Gerry Adams and Martin McGuinness, has shifted to a political track. Note the IRA's assertion of "total" commitment to a "permanent peace." Present the Adams argument: republicanism has given up its constitutional claim, is running the country, and talks with the British government. Acknowledge that their U-turn is credible because of how long they resisted it. Explain their view that decommissioning is achievable but beside the point—the de facto end of violence is what matters.

Section 2: The Unionist Position. Argue that unionists have shown equal sincerity. Note they have accepted republicans into power and started running the government together. Mention they have overcome vast obstacles in the unionist psyche. Acknowledge pressure from hate-filled supporters and parts of the London press hoping for failure. Credit David Trimble with statesmanship for maneuvering past historical negatives. Note unionists have already lived with procrastination over decommissioning and will swallow most of the Patten report on the RUC.

Section 3: The Outsider's Temptation. Present the view of outsiders not steeped in Ulster politics: that unionists should take one more stride, even without decommissioning by May. Pose the question: having swallowed the camel, are unionists straining at the gnat? Address the argument that insisting on Trimble's resignation if decommissioning isn't delivered puts blood on unionist hands.

Conclusion: Reject the notion that it is insensitive to talk about blame. Assert that in the hour of crisis, it is the republicans who are on trial. Argue that Realpolitik has reached its limit. State that Sinn Fein-IRA must take a "decisive—though entirely incomplete—surrender." Explain that a token handover is substantively meaningless but politically crucial. Conclude that in the real world, a promise is not enough—the "unbearable discomfort of a necessary gesture" is required as proof that republicanism has entered the world it chose to belong to. End by characterizing everything else as "bunker piety."

Allude to the following references: Gerry Adams, Martin McGuinness, David Trimble, General de Chastelaine, the Good Friday agreement, the May 22 deadline, and the Patten report on the RUC.

author: Jonathan Freedland
title: cross_topic_1/test/3/test_22
assignment:
Write an opinion essay of approximately 1,133 words analyzing the IRA's July 2002 apology to "non-combatant" victims of the Troubles, examining whether it represents genuine moral progress or cynical political maneuvering within the Northern Ireland peace process.

**Opening:** Begin with a personal analogy: apologies are often easier to give than to receive. The one who apologizes claims the moral high ground; the recipient is trapped—unready to forgive but damned as obstinate if they refuse. Scale this up from the kitchen table to Northern Ireland.

**The Apology Itself:** Describe the IRA's statement—its language of psychotherapy ("hurt," "pain," "grief"), its notable refusal to blame the British government, and its focus on "non-combatants." Note it was prompted by the approaching anniversary of Bloody Friday (1972).

**First Reactions:** Show the split among unionists. Some, like Tom Donnelly (whose sister died on Bloody Friday), were moved and hopeful. Most were cynical, seeing it as "pure PR."

**Political Stakes:** Explain the looming deadline: the following Wednesday, the British government must declare whether the IRA is honoring its ceasefire. If not, David Trimble will demand Sinn Féin's expulsion from the Northern Ireland executive. Frame the apology as potentially timed to influence this verdict.

**Unionist Disillusionment:** Detail why unionist faith has collapsed. The 1998 Good Friday Agreement was always seen by many as a "moral outrage," tolerated only in hope republicans would leave violence behind. The Colombia arrests (three republicans with FARC), the Castlereagh break-in, and ongoing Belfast street violence destroyed that hope. Note that if assembly elections were held now, Ian Paisley's anti-agreement DUP would overwhelm Trimble's UUP.

**What Unionists Want:** Describe their desire for a US envoy—appointed by George W. Bush—to assess ceasefire compliance, a move they believe would reassure their base and buy Trimble time.

**Republican Counter-Position:** Republicans reject anything not explicitly in the Good Friday Agreement with "jesuitical rigour." They dismiss the US envoy idea and any "widening" of the ceasefire. They expect Blair to deliver a verdict ambiguous enough to keep Sinn Féin in government. Note the choreographed quality of the timing between IRA, London, and Dublin.

**Republican Defense:** Acknowledge this was a "big deal"—hard for a self-styled liberation movement that believes its cause was just. Recall how recently Sinn Féin could barely express regret; now the IRA itself apologizes.

**Thought Experiment:** Project to September 2031. Imagine al-Qaida's ruling council issuing a statement of regret for 9/11, thirty years on. Ask: would it heal the victims' families? Probably not. But if al-Qaida had been on ceasefire for six to eight years, wouldn't the world feel relieved by such a statement? Wouldn't it signal something about intent?

**Conclusion:** Acknowledge the apology's limits—it should have included "combatants" (RUC, army) too. But ask: what sinister purpose were these "ploys" actually serving? Only to keep republicans in government, as the agreement allows. Decommissioning and the apology alike serve one end: keeping the peace process afloat now that republicans have decided it serves their strategic interest. Close with the image of republicanism on a slow, incomplete journey from paramilitary past to political future—"not neat or perfect, but more real with each passing month." End on the parallel: in peace processes, as in life, sometimes you must know how to say yes.

author: Jonathan Freedland
title: cross_topic_1/test/3/test_25
assignment:
Write an opinion essay of approximately 1,700 words arguing that Britain's unwritten constitution is broken and must be replaced with a new written constitutional settlement. The essay should adopt the stance of a progressive political commentator writing around 2000, addressing a general but politically engaged readership.

**Thesis:** The traditional defense of Britain's unwritten constitution — that it has worked for centuries and therefore should not be touched — no longer holds. The system is failing, as evidenced by repeated governance disasters, collapsing public trust, and Labour's own partial reforms exposing contradictions that demand resolution. A new, written constitutional settlement is urgently needed to prevent the union from unraveling and to equip Britain for a world grappling with questions of sovereignty.

**Introduction:** Open with the familiar conservative refrain — "if it ain't broke, don't fix it" — as the historic defense of the unwritten constitution. Acknowledge the argument that the patchwork of custom and tradition has endured, then pivot sharply: the system is now demonstrably broken. Establish the core test of any constitution as the quality of governance it produces.

**Section 1 — Evidence of Failure:** Catalogue a series of governance calamities from the mid-1980s through 2000: the pension scandal, BSE crisis, parliamentary sleaze, railway privatization, the poll tax, and the Millennium Dome debacle. Argue these were not isolated errors but systemic failures — the constitution lacked the checks, balances, and scrutiny mechanisms to prevent them. Reference the Scott inquiry (arms-for-Iraq) and the Phillips report (BSE) as authoritative confirmations that the flaw lies in the system of governance itself, not in particular individuals or administrations.

**Section 2 — Collapse of Public Confidence:** Present polling data showing declining faith in institutions. Include specific statistics: confidence in the House of Commons halving between 1985 and 1995; a 1996 EU poll ranking Britons' faith in parliament near the bottom of member states; collapsing local election turnout; a Mori survey showing 71% of first-time voters believing their ballot would make little or no difference. Frame declining voter turnout — including a Leeds by-election with under 20% participation — as citizens voting with their feet.

**Section 3 — Labour's Reforms Have Changed Everything:** Argue that since 1997, Labour has undertaken constitutional changes (devolution to Scotland, Wales, and Northern Ireland; partial House of Lords reform) so radical that historians must reach back to the 1830s for precedent. The conservative myth of an untouchable, mystical constitution has been shattered. The patchwork quilt is already unraveling — which means it can and must be renewed.

**Section 4 — The Four Nations Dilemma (subheading: "Four Nations"):** Explore how devolution has forced Britain to confront its multi-national identity. Britain acted as a unitary state governed from Westminster; devolution has exposed it as a nascent federal entity. But Labour's decentralization has opened questions without resolving them: What about England? Should Westminster serve as a federal legislature or double as an English parliament? Why does Scotland have more autonomy than other nations? Articulate the West Lothian question — why should Scottish MPs vote on English matters when English MPs cannot vote on Scottish ones? The worms were always in the can; now the can is open.

**Section 5 — The House of Lords Exposed (subheading: "Shattered Mystique"):** Use House of Lords reform as the clearest illustration. Removing most hereditary peers was meant to solve the second chamber's problems; instead it exposed the democratic deficit. When hereditaries dominated, the Lords could be dismissed as a Ruritanian joke. Now Britons see a supposedly reformed chamber in which not a single member is elected. The old Tory position of leaving well alone is dead; once you tinker, you must do it properly and democratically. Note even William Hague and the Conservatives are now flirting with radical constitutional ideas like an English parliament. The current hybrid of old custom and New Labour modernization risks being the worst of both worlds.

**Section 6 — The Global Context:** Argue that Britain's constitutional crisis coincides with profound external upheavals. Reference the upcoming EU summit in Nice debating the future shape of the European Union — majority voting vs. vetoes, common currency, shared military force, a basic law for Europe. Then widen the lens to the global anti-capitalist protests in Seattle and Prague, raising the question of who rules the world — people or corporations and the WTO. In every arena, the debate turns on sovereignty: who should be in charge? This is the question for the world, for Europe, and for Britain.

**Conclusion:** Synthesize the three pressures — the old system's failures, Labour's incomplete reforms, and the global sovereignty debate — into a single urgent call for a new constitutional settlement. Argue this settlement would improve governance, complete Labour's unfinished business, and prevent the union from unraveling. Contend that Britain cannot clarify its relationship with Europe or the world while remaining confused about its own internal sovereignty. Close by declaring that the moth-eaten garb of the past no longer fits; a new century and millennium demand a new, written constitution.

author: Catherine Bennett
title: cross_topic_1/test/0/test_90
assignment:
Write a satirical essay (approximately 1,100 words) in two main parts, linked by a shared skepticism toward institutions that claim moral authority while behaving questionably.

**Part One — City sexism and compensation culture:** Open with a mock-anthropological question about why intelligent, highly qualified women freely choose to work in the City of London, answering that whatever the motivation, the environment is brutish and sexist—though wryly note it is not as bad as police forces, fast-food chains, men's magazines, mini-brothels, or the Church of England Synod. Establish that public knowledge of this ghastliness comes from a parade of discrimination and unfair-dismissal claims, without which the City might still pose as a model equal-opportunities employer.

Trace a lineage of City complainants beginning with Nicola Horlick (1997), portrayed as a press-savvy martyr who reframed a grubby staff-poaching dispute into a moral crusade, dragging entranced reporters to Frankfurt and back. Note her rapid recovery and the way successors have demanded ever-larger sums to compensate for bullying, spitting, name-calling ("hot totty"), and lapdancing incidents. Introduce Claire Bright, seeking £11m from HBOS where she earned £600,000, having allegedly suffered under a man she called "a mini Hitler"—include the darkly comic observation that, given the size of a City office versus Nazi Germany, only a "mini-Holocaust" was achievable. Then cite six Dresdner Kleinwort Wasserstein bankers collectively demanding £800m for offences including being called "the Pamela Anderson of trading" and witnessing a CEO date his assistant.

Drive the satire to its peak by contrasting these figures with Criminal Injuries Compensation Board payouts for July 7 bombing victims who lost limbs for roughly £50,000 per leg. Question whether newspapers should even advertise such gargantuan claims, since they bear little relation to real-world settlements and may inflict further pain on the "still-traumatised" victims and their lawyers.

**Part Two — Faith schools and amateur education:** Pivot with a bold subheading asking whether schools can be protected from "forces of darkness." Discuss a Guardian advertisement placed by Oasis UK, the charity of Rev Steve Chalke, seeking an "accomplished fundraiser" to realise a vision of "outstanding schools and community hubs" rooted in Christian values. Note the jargon "HVD" (high value dealer) and observe that neither the fundraiser nor Chalke appears to need school-running experience. Broaden the critique: the government has decided children's education depends on amateurs—homophobic religious fanatics, self-promoting tradesmen, would-be barons, parents, classroom assistants—citing Peter Hyman (Blair's speechwriter who went to Islington Green "to do something different") and Reg Vardy (creationist school sponsor). End this section with a sarcastic question about whether children's minds will be safe in such hands.

**Coda — "This week":** Close with a brief personal column in which the narrator mentions reading Naomi Wolf's *The Treehouse* (prompting a desire to revisit *Little House in the Big Woods* for superior life and cabin-building lessons from Pa) and shamefully enjoying George Galloway's self-destruction on *Celebrity Big Brother*.

Maintain a tone of mordant irony throughout; deploy mock-sympathy toward the high-earning complainants; let the structural contrast between City millions and bombing-victim thousands carry the moral argument without explicit sermonising.

author: George Monbiot
title: cross_topic_1/test/1/test_103
assignment:
Write an opinion essay arguing that Western media and public attention have catastrophically ignored the Congo conflict—the deadliest since World War II—because of a misplaced moral narrative that casts Rwanda as a permanent victim rather than an aggressor. Target length: approximately 1,200 words.

**Thesis:** The Rwandan government, once rightly seen as the victim of genocide, has become one of the world's worst perpetrators of war crimes and resource plunder in the eastern Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC), and the international community's refusal to acknowledge this—compounded by media distraction—is enabling the continued slaughter of millions.

**Introduction:**
- Open by contrasting the media's obsession with a political sex scandal (the Home Secretary's love affair) against the near-total silence surrounding the resumption of civil war in the DRC, a conflict already responsible for 3.8 million deaths.
- Note that the Rwandan army has crossed back into north-eastern DRC; rival Congolese army factions, some loyal to Rwanda, are fighting; civilians are being killed, raped, tortured, and forced to flee.
- Cite the International Rescue Committee's finding that even before the latest fighting, over 1,000 people were dying daily from disease and malnutrition caused by the previous conflict, nearly half under five.

**Section 1 — Rwanda's Historical Justification and Its Erosion:**
- Acknowledge that Rwanda's initial invasions of the DRC (formerly Zaire) had apparent justification: Interahamwe militias who killed 800,000 Rwandans in the 1994 genocide fled there, sheltered first by Mobutu, then by Kabila, and threatened to reinvade Rwanda.
- Explain the turning point: after the second invasion in 1998, Rwanda abandoned pursuit of the genocidaires in favor of exploiting minerals—diamonds and coltan.
- Cite a UN Security Council report finding that by 1999, 80% of Rwanda's military budget (~$320 million/year) came from stolen Congolese minerals.

**Section 2 — The Scale of Atrocity and Plunder:**
- Describe the six-nation African turf war over mines: millions displaced, thousands forced into mining or prostitution.
- Note Rwanda's operation was the most efficient, controlled directly from Kigali (per Amnesty International), and continued even after the official 2002 withdrawal.
- Characterize the latest invasion as a thinly disguised effort to neutralize militias threatening Rwanda's mineral business.

**Section 3 — The Moral Blind Spot:**
- Contrast Western concern for Darfur with indifference toward Congo.
- Explain why: the public is accustomed to seeing Rwandan forces as the "good guys"—genocide survivors who drove out the killers. It is psychologically difficult to accept that victims can become perpetrators, and some of the bloodiest war criminals.
- Address the counterargument that criticism of Paul Kagame's government is driven by UN Security Council power plays. Refute it by listing the independent organizations that reached the same conclusions: Amnesty International, Human Rights Watch, the International Crisis Group, Global Witness, the British all-party parliamentary group, and the US State Department.

**Section 4 — The Evidence:**
- Describe the reports: hundreds of pages, eyewitness accounts, testimony from survivors and perpetrators.
- Enumerate specific atrocities: rape of children as young as three; genital mutilation of resisting women; forced labor in mines with victims buried alive; torched villages, looting, killing of resisters, millions forced into the jungle.
- Note that most of the 3.8 million deaths were from malnutrition and disease, but the marauding armies bear equivalent responsibility.
- Emphasize the documentary specificity: named agents and victims, dates, locations, value of stolen resources, names of buyers.

**Section 5 — Refuting the "Disciplined Army vs. Chaotic Rebels" Defense:**
- Address the argument (citing journalist Victoria Brittain as an example) that critics confuse Rwanda's disciplined army with chaotic rebel groups.
- Counter with the US State Department's summary of "numerous credible reports" of regular Rwandan troops killing, torturing, and raping in Kivu and Maniema.
- Argue that there is no moral difference between killing directly and commissioning proxies to do so. Detail Rwanda's creation and sustained arming of the RCD-Goma militia—formed in Kigali, financed and equipped by Rwanda with heavy weapons (per Global Witness and Amnesty International).

**Section 6 — The Interahamwe Excuse:**
- Acknowledge that the DRC government in Kinshasa has also committed crimes, but note its authority barely runs in the east.
- Cite a UN report leaked to the BBC confirming Rwanda and its proxies are the dominant forces in eastern DRC, controlling most minerals and involved in nearly all fighting.
- Argue Rwanda could have destroyed the Interahamwe—now much weaker—years ago. Cite the International Crisis Group: Rwanda had total military control of eastern Congo from 1996–2002 and failed to neutralize them.
- Note the British parliamentary group's finding that Rwandan forces were often stationed where no Interahamwe existed, sometimes even allying with them to control mines.
- Conclude that Rwanda is using the old excuse to drag eastern Congo back into war.

**Conclusion:**
- Argue the international community could easily pressure Rwanda: a tiny, fragile state dependent on foreign aid, over a third of which comes from Britain.
- End with the moral imperative: nothing will change until the public wakes up to this war and stops pretending that victims of atrocious crimes cannot also be perpetrators.

**Tone and Style:** Indignant, forensic, morally urgent. Use specific numbers, named organizations, and documented atrocities to build an overwhelming evidentiary case. Balance moral outrage with structured argumentation. Include a closing URL line referencing the author's website.

author: George Monbiot
title: cross_topic_1/test/1/test_104
assignment:
**Writing Assignment**

Write an approximately 1,200-word essay arguing that the political project of George W. Bush's administration is best understood not as a form of fascism but as a revival of Puritanism. The essay should be provocative in tone, historically grounded, and aimed at correcting a popular misdiagnosis on the left.

**Opening:** Begin by citing the claim—made by writers such as Barbara Probst Solomon and Richard Sennett, and circulating widely on the internet—that Bush's America represents an emerging fascism, whether "soft" or full-blown. Dismiss this framing. Use Robert Paxton's definition from *The Anatomy of Fascism*—fascism as a politics of community decline, humiliation, victimhood, and compensatory cults of unity, energy, and purity—to show that Bush's project does not fit. Note that fascism was elite-allied but arose from hysterical popular mobilization, something Bush does not provoke. Then pivot: the Bush project is not unprecedented, but it repeats a different ideology entirely, and failing to recognize it prevents effective opposition.

**Section 1 — Puritanism Misunderstood:** Explain that Puritanism is widely misunderstood as merely a joyless cult of self-denial—church-stripping, entertainment-banning—which seems distant from Republican America's conspicuous consumption. Argue instead that Puritanism was the product of an economic transformation.

**Section 2 — The Feudal State as Regulator:** Describe how, in early 17th-century England, the remnants of the feudal state functioned analogously to 20th-century social democracy: it served monarchy and clergy but also regulated economic exploitation. Reference R.H. Tawney's *Religion and the Rise of Capitalism* (1926) and Charles I's efforts to nationalize industries, control foreign exchange, and prosecute lords who evicted peasants, employers who underpaid workers, and magistrates who denied poor relief.

**Section 3 — The Economic Revolution:** Explain why this model collapsed. Over 150 years, international commercial companies concentrated financial power on an unprecedented scale, subjecting medieval industrial organization to a new money-power. Commerce and finance, not industry, drove the transformation. Monarchs became puppets of financiers. In England, the dissolution of the monasteries catalyzed a massive seizure of wealth by a new commercial class—land enclosure, mass eviction, land speculation, and sophisticated financial markets experimenting in futures and arbitrage, "all the vices we now associate with the Age of Enron."

**Section 4 — The Early Reformation vs. Late Puritanism:** Note that early Reformation theologians furiously denounced this exploitation, preaching charity, justice, and punishment of exploitation—a spirit that persisted among New England settlers but was crushed in England. Puritanism became the religion of the new commercial classes: traders, money lenders, bankers, industrialists. Calvin gave them what the old order could not: a theological justification of commerce. Capitalism could glorify God.

**Section 5 — The Ideological Inversion:** Trace the logical steps. From Calvin's doctrine of individual purification came an "idealisation of personal responsibility" before God, which became a theory of individual rights, reversing the traditional scheme of Christian virtues. By the mid-17th century, most English Puritans saw poverty as moral failure, not misfortune, and wealth as a blessing rewarding energy and will. Individualism in religion led to individualist morality, which led to the disparagement of the social fabric. Then the Puritans conflated religious and commercial callings: cite the preacher Richard Steele (1684) on the tradesman serving God in his calling. Business success became proof of spiritual grace. Joseph Lee anticipated Adam Smith's invisible hand by over a century, claiming the advancement of private persons serves the public—meaning men of property destroying everyone else's advancement.

**Section 6 — Administrative Nihilism:** Describe Tawney's characterization of Puritans as early converts to "administrative nihilism"—the minimal state. Business affairs should be settled by businessmen, unhampered by antiquated morality. They formulated a new theory of social obligation: helping the poor created idleness and spiritual dissolution. Note the one difference from Bush's people: Puritans worshipped production, not consumption—but this is the same disease with a different symptom. Quote Tawney: the world exists not to be enjoyed but to be conquered; only its conqueror deserves the name of Christian.

**Section 7 — Suppression and Distraction:** Mention the Levellers and Diggers, who remained true to the original Reformation spirit, but were violently suppressed. The pursuit of adulterers and sodomites served as a distraction for the impoverished lower classes. Reference Ronan Bennett's novel *Havoc in its Third Year* (set in the 1630s) as a parable: obsession with terrorists (Irish and Jesuit), homosexuality, sexual license, vicious punishment of moral deviance, and disparagement of public support for the poor—swap black suits for grey, and the characters walk out of Bush's America.

**Conclusion:** Answer the question: why has this ideology resurfaced in 2004? Because it must. The enrichment of the elite and the impoverishment of the lower classes require a justifying ideology to be sustained. In the United States, that ideology must be religious. Bush's government is driven back to Puritanism by historical necessity. Close with the line: to understand what we are confronting, we must look not to the 1930s but to the 1630s.

author: George Monbiot
title: cross_topic_1/test/1/test_105
assignment:
Write an opinion essay arguing that the International Atomic Energy Agency's dual mission—to prevent nuclear weapons proliferation while simultaneously spreading civilian nuclear technology—is an irreconcilable contradiction that actually accelerates proliferation. Your essay should run approximately 1,150 words.

**Opening:** Begin with a short satirical limerick or rhyme mocking the IAEA head's impossible position, then introduce the central paradox: the agency is tasked with taking away nuclear weapons capability with one hand while distributing the materials and expertise to build it with the other. Reference Mohamed El Baradei and his predecessor Hans Blix. Note that this contradiction recently allowed Iran to dismiss IAEA demands.

**Section 1 — Origins of the contradiction:** Trace the agency's founding to Eisenhower's 1953 "Atoms for Peace" speech to the United Nations. Capture the genuine optimism of the era: cite a contemporaneous newspaper article promising atomic cars, the end of coal mining, and an "earthly paradise." Quote Eisenhower's language about beating the nuclear sword into a ploughshare and distributing fissionable materials to "power-starved" regions as an incentive to forswear weapons. Explain that the IAEA's statute and the Non-Proliferation Treaty both encode this dual purpose: prevent weapons, spread technology.

**Section 2 — The dream collapses:** Argue that this program made sense only while the dream was still dreamtable. Assert that every state that developed nuclear weapons in the past 30 years—Israel, South Africa, India, Pakistan, North Korea, Iraq—did so by diverting resources from civilian nuclear programs, sometimes with direct Atoms for Peace assistance. Detail how India's bomb was built with help from Canada, the US, Germany, France, Norway, and the UK, and how Pakistan received parallel assistance. Frame this as equipping nations for total war in the name of peace. Note that roughly 20 countries now could become weapons states within months thanks to foreign civilian nuclear assistance, and that Russia was not just permitted but *obligated* under the NPT to ship uranium and bomb-making technology to Iran.

**Section 3 — The Iran case:** Explain that Iran is enriching uranium and producing heavy water—processes legitimate for power generation but easily repurposed for weapons (enriching to bomb-grade requires only additional centrifuge passes). Argue that the NPT gives Iran both the materials and the legal cover for a weapons program: if you want a bomb, you sign the treaties, join the IAEA, and use your entitlements.
