Then lay out Iran's motives: Israel's nuclear arsenal and US-protected refusal to sign the NPT, the US and UK abandoning their own disarmament obligations and considering pre-emptive use, American military bases surrounding Iran, and the lesson of North Korea—that having the bomb grants impunity. Conclude that if Iran is *not* seeking weapons, it has misunderstood global politics.

**Section 4 — El Baradei's helplessness:** Note that El Baradei can only beg Iran to accept "voluntary agreements" because the treaty he enforces gives him no authority to stop legally permitted enrichment. Paint the danger: official nuclear powers have junked the NPT while non-nuclear powers exploit it, raising the specter of a Middle East nuclear cascade—Hezbollah striking Israel, Israel turning on Iran, and a second nuclear arms race pulling in everyone else.

**Conclusion:** Argue the obvious but unspoken conclusion: you cannot phase out nuclear weapons without phasing out nuclear power. Call for a new nuclear treaty based on grim realities rather than Eisenhower's utopian fantasy, and end with a sharp line about the IAEA head destroying the world in order to save it.

author: George Monbiot
title: cross_topic_1/test/1/test_106
assignment:
Write an opinion essay of approximately 1200 words arguing that progressive voters should not settle for a "lesser evil" Democratic presidential candidate but should instead support a third-party progressive candidate, even in a close election against an incumbent president widely seen as dangerous. Use the 2004 US presidential election as your case study, with John Kerry as the compromised Democrat, George W. Bush as the incumbent, and Ralph Nader as the progressive alternative.

Open with a rhetorical question: why does a country full of brilliant people so often end up choosing between two unimpressive men? Answer bluntly—because unimpressive men keep getting elected—and note that this year the progressive establishment is once again instructing voters to choose the safer, duller option.

Lay out the progressive establishment's case honestly and with some sympathy. Mention that nearly every major progressive voice—the Guardian, the Nation magazine, even Noam Chomsky—has urged swing-state voters to back Kerry. Summarize their logic: Kerry may be uninspiring—"about as inspiring as a parking lot on a wet Sunday in Detroit"—but he is the only one who can defeat Bush, and the race is so tight that even a few thousand Nader votes in swing states could hand Bush the election. Note that Republicans have even funneled money to Nader's campaign for this reason. Concede that the argument has real merit.

Enumerate Bush's offenses in a substantial paragraph: two unnecessary wars, threats against dozens of nations, shredded international treaties and domestic regulations, corporate license to accelerate climate change, a global assault on civil liberties, and the dismantling of the idea that the state should tax the rich and help the poor. The world would be safer without him.

Quote progressive outlets making the "exceptional circumstances" argument. Attribute to a Guardian leader the claim that Kerry's flaws are overshadowed by Bush's neoconservative agenda and catastrophic war-making. Quote the Nation's open letter to Nader asserting that 2004 is not 2000 and that Bush's defeat is critical.

Then deliver the counter-punch: both publications said the same thing in 2000. Cite the Nation's Eric Alterman blaming Gore's defeat on Nader's "megalomania," and quote a pre-2000-election Guardian piece calling Gore the "safe, wise choice" despite being neither exciting nor visionary. Extend the pattern: similar warnings appear in nearly every modern presidential election. Argue that the US electoral system—built on patronage, corruption, and media fear—will always produce dreadful candidates and therefore always generate "exceptional circumstances." Only a leader willing to overturn the system and reintroduce democracy will end the cycle. Voting for the bad to stop the terrible in 2004 guarantees the same dilemma in 2008, and onward indefinitely.

Argue that such systemic change requires political courage: taking on the corporations that buy elections and the media that sets the boundaries of debate. Contrast Kerry's genuine military courage in Vietnam with his total absence of courage on the campaign trail. Cite his statement that "even knowing what we now know" he would still have voted to authorize the Iraq war. Report that his national security adviser, James Rubin, told the Washington Post that Kerry would "in all probability" have attacked Iraq by now if he were president. Frame Kerry's fundraising prowess not as democratic success but as corporate investment—backers fund him because they expect compliance, and they are unlikely to be wrong. Use the line: "When Kerry gets his orders, he reports for duty."

Dismiss the notion that Kerry could oversee democratic reform. Call him frightened and flinching, a man who made the system work for him by working for the system, who knows it would destroy him if he turned against it. Contrast him unfavorably with Hugo Chávez, noting Kerry lacks charisma or popular enthusiasm. Conclude this section: a vote for Kerry is not merely a vote against Bush but a vote for the survival of the system that made Bush possible.

Pivot to Nader. Acknowledge disagreements with some of his positions—on trade, for instance—but insist no one can deny his courage. Call his decision to run despite near-universal progressive opposition both brave and foolhardy. Note his career fighting corporations and enduring media attacks, and highlight his willingness to take on the Anti-Defamation League, which he accuses of smearing critics of Israeli policy as antisemites—something no other US politician has dared to do.

Concede Nader will not win. Reframe: the point is that systemic change must begin now, not in an endlessly postponed future, and the better Nader performs, the faster the movement for change will grow.

Respond to the Nation's suggestion that Nader would have more impact as a "public citizen" advocating for open debates and rallying voters behind progressive Democrats. Counter: what incentive would Democrats have to listen? He holds leverage over these "cowering creatures" only while they fear he will take their votes.

Acknowledge differences between Kerry and Bush in a nuanced paragraph. Bush is active, Kerry passive. Kerry wants high defense spending but would be more reluctant to attack other nations. He wants tax cuts but is less willing to gut health and education. He wants lower oil prices but won't help corporations open the Arctic Wildlife Refuge.

End with the central question: is this difference enough to justify abandoning the only current electoral effort to democratize US politics? Answer no. Frame the progressive establishment's plea as asking voters to trade liberty for security and democracy for expediency. Close with a direct instruction: the voters should ignore them.

author: Hugo Young
title: cross_topic_1/test/2/test_119
assignment:
Write a first-person essay (approximately 959 words) from the perspective of a long-time foreign visitor to the United States who, despite years of study, reporting, and personal ties (including marriage to an American), realizes he never truly knew the country until the post-9/11 moment. Spend Christmas in a remote, liberal corner of Vermont and use that vantage point to generalize about the nation.

**Thesis:** America has not changed since 9/11; it has become "more so." Each national virtue and vice has intensified, clarified, and stripped itself of ambiguity.

**Introduction:** Establish your long familiarity with America — study, reporting, connections, marriage — then deliver the turn: you did not know it. Suggest that perhaps America did not fully know itself until 9/11 and the Afghan war forced it into self-examination. State the thesis: nothing has changed, but everything is "more so."

**Body sections, each treating a national trait that has intensified:**

1. **Consideration.** Americans were always considerate; now they are "super-caring." The attack was collectively shared. Three months on, the shock still reverberates; no stoical sloughing-off has occurred. People still blunder about searching for meaning, and their response is to care more tenderly.

2. **Self-confidence.** No whining, no self-pity. Self-sufficiency as second nature. American certainties — maddening to bin Laden — have redoubled. Note the return of humor after a period of solemnity, citing Garrison Keillor's jihad jokes as evidence that Islam has been dropped from political correctness.

3. **Patriotism.** Flags always hung in Vermont yards, but now patriotism is far more intense. Note the irony: Vermont sends a self-described socialist to Congress and has little affection for George W. Bush, yet the patriotic response to 9/11 is unquestioning. Use local detail: a tourist-free area with very local papers (cite the Valley News of White River Junction) that now leads with Hamid Karzai's swearing-in and carries news of Yasser Arafat's Bethlehem visit. Clarify: this is not new internationalism but a shedding of indifference toward the world the threat comes from. America's role is simply to protect America — and that is nothing to be ashamed of.

4. **Constitutionalism.** Here the change is more marked and more troubling. Even liberal Vermont acquaintances show only modest objection to Bush's military tribunals and curtailments of due process. The government cites Lincoln and FDR as precedent for suspending habeas corpus and free speech in times of threat. This seems to satisfy people.

5. **Domestic politics vs. foreign policy.** Note that domestic politics continues normally — budget arguments, left and right, taxation debates, positioning for 2002 elections. But in foreign affairs, politics has virtually ceased. America has become "easy to lead. Alarmingly so." Even Bush detrusters trust him because there seems no alternative. He vows to "get the job done," that archetypal American phrase.

**Conclusion:** Capture the casual fatalism — friends joking about being "blown to pieces." The sense that fate has taken over and America must do what it must do: extinguish al-Qaida. American idealism, historically fickle in post-war diplomacy, now has a purpose everyone agrees on: saving America itself. End with the observation that it is unclear what this means for the world, but clearer than it has been since Jefferson what the world means for America: "safety from sea to shining sea, and maybe not beyond."

**Tone:** Observational, affectionate but clear-eyed, generalizing from a small corner while acknowledging the audacity of doing so. Balance personal anecdote with national character analysis.

author: Jonathan Freedland
title: cross_topic_1/test/3/test_127
assignment:
Write an essay of approximately 1256 words reflecting on the 60th anniversary of the founding of Israel. Use a specific framing device to bridge the past and present: late-night reruns of old Israeli television specials featuring kibbutz choirs singing patriotic Hebrew folk melodies. Your essay should argue that while Israel is often misunderstood by its modern critics, its current society has drifted far from its founding ideals, resulting in a dangerous complacency regarding the peace process. 

Structure your essay as follows:

**Introduction**
Begin by describing the old black-and-white television reruns of kibbutz choirs—men in work shirts, women in peasant skirts—singing to a smiling audience. Note that as Israel celebrates its 60th anniversary with modern street parties and beach barbecues, these old, cheesy socialist-realist tableaus actually offer a profound window into understanding what Israel was and what it has become. State your thesis: Israel was not founded as an imperialist project but as a desperate refuge for a stateless people, yet today's Israelis have retreated from their collectivist ideals into a privatized "bubble," losing their urgency for peace.

**Section 1: Refugees, Not Imperialists**
Address the common critique that Israel was merely an act of western imperialism, inserting a European enclave into the Middle East, comparable to white settlers in Australia or South Africa. Rebut this by pointing out the immense ethnic diversity of modern Jewish Israelis (Moroccan, Russian, Ethiopian, Kurd). Deepen this counter-argument by referencing the Zionist anthem "Ein Li Eretz Acheret" ("I Have No Other Land"), noting the tragic symmetry that Palestinians feel the same way. Explain that the Jewish state was established just three years after the liberation of Auschwitz because the world finally recognized the Jews' basic right to a home. To illustrate this, recount the story of Yossi Harel, captain of the *Exodus*, which carried 4,500 Holocaust survivors to Palestine in 1947 only to be turned back by the British and sent to Germany. Draw a moral distinction between the pioneers of America, Africa, and Australasia (landowners seeking a second home or resources) and the Jewish refugees of the 1940s (a homeless man desperate for a roof).

**Section 2: The Socialist Ethos**
Use the old TV shows to transition into a discussion of early Israel's political economy. Argue that early Israel was less like Rhodesia and more like East Germany—a small country where socialism was the state religion. Provide specific examples of this collectivism: mass-produced identical floor tiles, a single kibbutz making every toilet seat, and foreign investors being required to sell a 51% stake in their companies to the Histadrut (Israel's trade union congress). 

**Section 3: Privatization and the "Bubble"**
Explain how this collectivism has vanished. The kibbutzim have privatized, with members owning their own homes and earning different wages. Use the kibbutz as a metaphor for wider Israeli society, which is also privatizing as people withdraw from the collective sphere. Introduce the concept of the "bu'ah"—the bubble Israelis construct to hide from the anxiety of the country's security situation. Cite polling data showing the dichotomy of the Israeli mindset: nearly 40% believe the country faces a serious threat of destruction, yet 83% are satisfied with their own personal lives. 

**Section 4: Complacency and the Peace Process**
Connect the 60th anniversary to the political deadline set by the US administration for progress in the Annapolis peace talks ahead of President George W. Bush's visit. Note Israel's insistence that it is striving for peace, referencing Foreign Minister Tzipi Livni's pride in continuing talks with Palestinians even on "days of terror," and Prime Minister Ehud Olmert's hawkish critics who claim his peace efforts are just a distraction from a corruption inquiry. Argue, however, that there is no genuine Israeli urgency for an accord. Point out that when Jimmy Carter visited Jerusalem carrying messages from Hamas and Damascus, no frontline Israeli minister would meet him. Note the contradiction: while Israel claims it cannot legitimize Hamas for fear of undermining Palestinian President Mahmoud Abbas, Olmert has done nothing to bolster Abbas, such as removing illegal West Bank outposts or compensating settlers to leave. Explain the cynical national mood: Israelis point to the withdrawals from Lebanon (2000) and Gaza (2005) which only brought Katyushas from Hezbollah and Qassams from Hamas. Conclude this section by noting that Israelis believe they can survive without peace because the separation barrier has contained terrorism, and the "demographic threat" of Arabs outnumbering Jews feels too abstract to force action. 

**Conclusion**
Conclude by contrasting the upcoming anniversary celebrations—fueled by a strong economy with 5.1% growth—with the historical memory of the old TV shows. End with the image of only a few insomniacs watching those old reruns, listening to a melody that sounds more passé now than ever: "Shir L'shalom," the song for peace.

author: Martin Kettle
title: cross_topic_1/test/4/test_132
assignment:
Write an essay of approximately 1,144 words comparing the American and British approaches to senior judicial appointments, arguing that however messy and politicized the US process becomes, it at least acknowledges the centrality of judges to democratic governance — whereas Britain's system remains in damaging denial about the constitutional importance of its judiciary.

**Introduction:** Open with a personal anecdote about arriving as a Washington correspondent and receiving advice from the late Alistair Cooke that the hardest thing for British people to grasp about the American political system is how important judges and courts are within it. Frame this as illustrative of the deceptive belief that Britain and America see the world through the same eyes. Then pivot to current events: note that the memory was recalled not only because John McCain was delivering the first Alistair Cooke memorial lecture in London, but because George Bush was crossing the Atlantic for a G8 summit — yet the topic dominating Washington had nothing to do with global poverty, climate change, or Iraq.

**Section 1 — The American Vacancy as Seismic Event:** Discuss the resignation of Justice Sandra Day O'Connor over the Fourth of July weekend. Establish that this is not merely a major Washington story but a potentially seismic national political event — at least as important, in its own context, as electing a president or a new Congress. Explain the structural centrality of the nine-justice Supreme Court to US government, the 11-year gap since the last nomination, and O'Connor's particular significance as the swing voter on crucial decisions for the past decade and a half — shaping the lives of millions of Americans on issues such as abortion, race, and religion.

**Section 2 — The Political Stakes:** Explain why the right sees this as an immense opportunity and the left as a moment of direst threat. Reference the open secret that Republican lobbies have prioritized reshaping the court under Antonin Scalia in an aggressively conservative mould, with a view to dismantling the role of the federal government since at least the New Deal. Note that Bush, with no election to face, may share this ambition. Then introduce countervailing constraints: most Americans are decent and pragmatic, offended by the right's culture wars (as opinion polls show and as the Terry Schiavo case recently reminded them); Republican candidates in the coming mid-term elections may be uneasy about nominating a culture-warrior judge; and Bush will face pressure to nominate another woman, given his pattern of not ignoring the political middle ground where O'Connor's cautious pragmatism played so well.

**Section 3 — The British Contrast:** Pivot sharply. Pose a general knowledge question: who is Britain's newly appointed lord chief justice, taking over from Lord Woolf on October 1? Provide the answer — Nicholas Phillips, Lord Phillips of Worth Matravers, presiding over the court of appeal for the past five years — and note that most readers will not know. Mention the further appointments: Sir Anthony Clarke as master of the rolls and Sir Igor Judge (flag this as surely the best name on the English bench) as head of the Queen's Bench Division. Describe how these three major appointments were announced quietly in a press release with almost no information about the appointees, received only cursory media attention, appeared on no front pages, prompted no follow-up coverage, and generated no public debate about whether these were the right people, where they stood on significant legal issues, or where these appointments might take the English courts.

**Section 4 — The Significance of the Silence:** Argue that this is striking and eloquent, especially given that these were the first appointments under the major changes brought in by the Constitutional Reform Act 2005. It might have been expected that such appointments would be marked in a way signalling to the public that these are key figures in a new constitutional era. In the event, nothing of the sort occurred. The most senior appointments in the supposedly reformed judicial system were made as they always have been — unheralded and with minimal openness.

**Conclusion:** Return to America. Note the reported fifty million dollars expected to be spent in the battle for O'Connor's seat, with two other justices in their 80s and a third a cancer sufferer — meaning the process may soon be repeated with greater intensity, expense, and epochal consequences. Acknowledge that from a distance this can seem a travesty, even a corruption, of a process for selecting senior members of an independent, merit-based judiciary. But insist that the American process at least explicitly and implicitly recognises the centrality of judges and the law to the constitutional processes of modern democratic government. By contrast, Britain — with rare and distinguished exceptions such as Lord Woolf — remains overwhelmingly in denial about these connections. Close by arguing that if citizens meekly concede they have no right to know about the highest judges in the land or to call their thinking to public account, they vacate an important part of the public arena. End on the chain of reasoning: unless we can respect judges, it is harder to respect the law; and unless we respect the law, it is harder to respect the system of government of which both law and judges are integral parts.

author: Martin Kettle
title: cross_topic_1/test/4/test_138
assignment:
Write an essay of approximately 1233 words analyzing the transformation of the modern American Republican Party, using the 2004 Republican National Convention as the focal point.

Begin by contrasting the historical GOP—once divided between a grand "compassionate conservative" wing (Theodore Roosevelt, Nelson Rockefeller) and a more modest wing (Dwight Eisenhower, Richard Nixon)—with the current party. Argue that today's GOP is an alliance between a dominant, aggressive religious conservative activist wing and a moderate electorate that mistakenly believes the party is benign.

Detail the orchestrated hostility at the 2004 convention, specifically the personal attacks on John Kerry. Mention the delegates wearing Purple Heart band-aids to mock Kerry's Vietnam wounds, and the brutal platform speeches, citing Georgia Senator Zell Miller's characterization of Kerry as "wrong, weak and wobbly." Contrast this with the non-combatant status of Bush and Cheney (who icily noted he had "other priorities" during Vietnam). Argue that this hypocrisy reveals the GOP's true goal: exclusive ownership of defense and security politics, rather than reverence for military service.

Note the total absence of criticism of the military at the convention, even regarding Abu Ghraib. Speculate that if Al Gore had been president during 9/11, Republicans would not have rallied around him but would have blamed Bill Clinton. Cite the prevalence of books blaming Clinton for 9/11 found in a New York Barnes and Noble (e.g., "Losing Bin Laden," "Dereliction of Duty," "Intelligence Failure"). Warn that a President Kerry would face a vindictive Republican opposition and an immediate impeachment reflex.

Frame the modern GOP as the political expression of Eisenhower's warning about the "misplaced power" of the military-industrial complex. Characterize the party as militarist, fueled by testosterone-driven "U-S-A" chants.

Pivot to the party's fusion of military-industrial power with religious imperatives. Discuss Bush's "double-coding" technique—using language that sounds moderate but signals to the religious right. Cite Senator Sam Brownback's declaration of a "culture war" at a private rally, and the convention's closing prayer asking for guidance against the "forces of evil." Conclude by noting that while much of the convention was Orwellian double-speak, the most ominous statements meant exactly what they said.
